result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen hasn't worked since March. I keep it on the counter anyway, next to the coffee maker and the ceramic rooster my mother gave me when I moved into this apartment seven years ago. The rooster is chipped now, one of its painted tail feathers cracked off during a dinner party I barely remember. I swept the piece under the refrigerator and never retrieved it. Sometimes I think about it down there in the dust.

Seven years is a long time to live somewhere and still feel like you're borrowing it.

I used to tell myself I'd leave when things got better, which was a way of never leaving, which was a way of never defining what better meant. Better was always a city I hadn't visited yet, a version of myself I hadn't assembled. Better was theoretical, clean, waiting patiently in some future that kept backing away as I walked toward it.

My neighbor plays the same three songs on a guitar every Saturday morning. I've learned them by proximity. I could hum all three without thinking. We have never spoken about this. We have barely spoken at all, only the hallway nods, the elevator silences, the brief negotiations over a package delivered to the wrong door. And yet I know something about her that feels almost intimate — the way she pauses between the second and third song, a silence that lasts about four minutes, long enough that I always wonder if she's done, and then she begins again.

I think a lot of what we know about people comes from the sounds they make when they think no one is listening.

There's a particular quality to early morning light in October that I have never found the right words for. It comes through the east window at an angle that makes ordinary things look briefly significant — the coffee mug, the stack of books I've been meaning to read, my own hands holding nothing. The light is thin and serious, like it has something to say but isn't sure you're worth saying it to. October always feels like a conversation I'm on the edge of understanding.

I walked to the park yesterday and sat on a bench for forty minutes without checking my phone. This felt like an accomplishment I couldn't tell anyone about without it sounding either pretentious or sad. A dog came and pressed its nose against my knee, just for a moment, before its owner pulled it away and apologized. I said it was fine. I meant it.

What I wanted to say was *thank you, actually.* What I wanted to say was *that was the best part of my week* and I wanted to say it without irony, which is harder than it sounds.

We have built such elaborate systems for not saying what we mean. Not out of cruelty — I don't think it's cruelty, mostly. Out of the reasonable fear that what we mean is too small or too large for the available space. That the other person will either shrug or be overwhelmed, and both outcomes feel equally lonely.

So we say *fine* and *no worries* and *sounds good* and we mean, sometimes, *I am very tired* and *I forgive you* and *I don't know how to be around people today but I'm trying.*

The broken radio — I should explain that. A storm in March knocked out power for six hours, and when it came back the radio made a sound like something tearing and then went quiet. I tried replacing the batteries. I tried plugging it in. I tried the thing where you leave it alone and come back later, which works for computers sometimes and for people more often than we admit.

Nothing. Just the faint impression of what a radio is supposed to be.

I keep it because throwing it away would require a decision, and some days decisions feel like a currency I'm running low on. Better to let the counter hold it. Better to walk past it every morning and not think too hard about what it means to keep a broken thing around because you remember when it worked.

I think that's enough about the radio.

The ceramic rooster still stands. Still watches the counter with its painted eyes. My mother chose it because she knows I don't have real plants, don't have pets, don't have — in her careful phrasing — *much going on in the decoration department*. The rooster is her way of saying *I want there to be something alive in your kitchen* while respecting my limitations. I have come to love it fiercely and privately, the way I love most things.

Outside right now the wind is doing something interesting to the trees. The last of the leaves are going. There's a specific melancholy to watching something end slowly that you can't quite explain to someone who hasn't sat with it. It doesn't feel like loss, exactly. It feels more like permission.

Permission to let the year close. To stop expecting so much from the remaining days of it. To sit in the kitchen with a cold cup of coffee and a broken radio and a chipped ceramic rooster and the distant sound of three songs you've learned by accident, and to think:

*This is a life. It's mine. I'm still figuring out what to do with it.*

The October light says nothing. The trees keep losing their leaves. I drink the cold coffee anyway.
